emotional_tone.closing: calmer but still raw, somewhat destabilised
emotional_tone.arc: distressed peak mid-session, partial stabilisation toward close

key_moments.content: User disclosed she tried to have the workload conversation with her manager but it escalated — manager accused her of 'not being a team player' and threatened to put her on a performance improvement plan
User has not slept properly in four days since the confrontation. Reports waking at 3am replaying the conversation and spiralling into worst-case scenarios about losing her job
User expressed regret about attempting the conversation at all — 'I should have just kept my head down, I ruined everything' — and is questioning all the progress made in sessions 2 and 3

Therapist reframed: user did not create the problem, she revealed it. The manager's threat confirms the dynamic was real. User sat with this for a moment and said 'I think I knew that. I just needed someone to say it.'
User mentioned her mother called this week and she could not explain why she was upset — 'I just said work is fine.' She is isolating from support systems while in crisis.

unresolved_themes: manager confrontation and its professional consequences
sleep disruption and somatic anxiety symptoms
isolation from personal support network during crisis
risk of reverting to pre-therapy self-suppression patterns
progress_markers: User did attempt the manager conversation — she followed through on her commitment
Recognised mid-session that she is catastrophising rather than seeing situation clearly
user_commitments: Will text one person in her personal life this week — does not need to explain everything, just make contact
Will reread her values list when the 3am spiral starts
clinical_flags: Signs of shame-based cognitive distortion — attributing manager's behaviour to personal failure

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: Aisha — what happened? You sounded urgent in your message.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I did it. I had the conversation. And it was a disaster. He said I wasn't a team player. He mentioned a performance improvement plan. An actual PIP. I've been awake since Tuesday.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: Okay. Let's slow down. Tell me what happened — from the beginning.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I had my notes. I was calm. I said I wanted to discuss workload distribution because I felt it had become unsustainable. He immediately got defensive. Said I was the only one complaining. Said if I couldn't handle the expectations maybe this wasn't the right fit. Then he said he'd 'been keeping notes' on me. He said the word PIP. I smiled and said thank you for the feedback and then I walked to the bathroom and sat on the floor for ten minutes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: You kept your composure in that room. That took enormous strength, Aisha. And then you came here. I want to acknowledge that.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I shouldn't have said anything. I ruined everything. I should have just kept my head down. Two months of sessions, all this progress, and I walked straight into a wall.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: Can I say something that might feel uncomfortable?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Yes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: You didn't create this problem by speaking. You revealed a problem that was already there. His response — the defensiveness, the notes, the PIP threat — that is not the response of a manager who received reasonable feedback. That is the response of someone who has been managing through fear. You didn't ruin anything. You just got confirmation of what you already suspected.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I think I knew that. I just needed someone to say it. I've been up at 3am every night running the conversation over and over. Like if I replay it enough I'll find the version where I said it better.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: There is no better version that would have changed his response. You're searching for control in a situation where someone else's behaviour is the problem. That's exhausting, Aisha. Have you talked to anyone? Family, a friend?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: My mum called Thursday. I just said work was fine. I couldn't — I don't know how to explain it. I don't want them to worry.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: So you're managing the crisis alone.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I'm used to it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: I know. And that pattern — it's the same one we've been working on. Let me ask you something. If your sister was going through exactly this, would you want her to call you?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Immediately. Yes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: Then you're allowed the same thing. You don't have to explain the whole situation. Just make contact. Text one person this week. Not to debrief — just so someone knows you exist and you're having a hard time.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I can do that. One text. I can do that.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: And the 3am spirals — do you still have your values list?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: It's in my phone.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: Read it at 3am. Not to fix everything. Just to remember who you are before you let the spiral write the story.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Okay. I'm still scared. I don't know what's going to happen at work.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.text: That fear is reasonable. The situation is genuinely uncertain. But you are not the same person who walked in here two months ago hiding tears in a bathroom. That is real, even on a night like tonight.
